Enter KING JOHN, QUEEN ELINOR, PEMBROKE, ESSEX, SALISBURY, and others, with CHATILLON

2.play: KING JOHN: Now, say, Chatillon, what would France with us?

3.play: CHATILLON: Thus, after greeting, speaks the King of France

4.play: In my behavior to the majesty,

5.play: The borrow'd majesty, of England here.

6.play: QUEEN ELINOR: A strange beginning: 'borrow'd majesty!'

7.play: KING JOHN: Silence, good mother; hear the embassy.

8.play: CHATILLON: Philip of France, in right and true behalf

9.play: Of thy deceased brother Geffrey's son,

10.play: Arthur Plantagenet, lays most lawful claim

11.play: To this fair island and the territories,

12.play: To Ireland, Poictiers, Anjou, Touraine, Maine,

13.play: Desiring thee to lay aside the sword

14.play: Which sways usurpingly these several titles,

15.play: And put these same into young Arthur's hand,

16.play: Thy nephew and right royal sovereign.

17.play: KING JOHN: What follows if we disallow of this?

18.play: CHATILLON: The proud control of fierce and bloody war,

19.play: To enforce these rights so forcibly withheld.

20.play: KING JOHN: Here have we war for war and blood for blood,

21.play: Controlment for controlment: so answer France.

22.play: CHATILLON: Then take my king's defiance from my mouth,

23.play: The farthest limit of my embassy.

24.play: KING JOHN: Bear mine to him, and so depart in peace:

25.play: Be thou as lightning in the eyes of France;

26.play: For ere thou canst report I will be there,

27.play: The thunder of my cannon shall be heard:

28.play: So hence! Be thou the trumpet of our wrath

29.play: And sullen presage of your own decay.

30.play: An honourable conduct let him have:

32.play: QUEEN ELINOR: What now, my son! have I not ever said

33.play: How that ambitious Constance would not cease

34.play: Till she had kindled France and all the world,

35.play: Upon the right and party of her son?

36.play: This might have been prevented and made whole

37.play: With very easy arguments of love,

38.play: Which now the manage of two kingdoms must

40.play: KING JOHN: Our strong possession and our right for us.

41.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Your strong possession much more than your right,

42.play: Or else it must go wrong with you and me:

43.play: So much my conscience whispers in your ear,

44.play: Which none but heaven and you and I shall hear.

45.play: ESSEX: My liege, here is the strangest controversy

46.play: Come from country to be judged by you,

47.play: That e'er I heard: shall I produce the men?

49.play: Our abbeys and our priories shall pay

52.play: BASTARD: Your faithful subject I, a gentleman

53.play: Born in Northamptonshire and eldest son,

54.play: As I suppose, to Robert Faulconbridge,

55.play: A soldier, by the honour-giving hand

56.play: Of Coeur-de-lion knighted in the field.

58.play: ROBERT: The son and heir to that same Faulconbridge.

59.play: KING JOHN: Is that the elder, and art thou the heir?

60.play: You came not of one mother then, it seems.

61.play: BASTARD: Most certain of one mother, mighty king;

62.play: That is well known; and, as I think, one father:

63.play: But for the certain knowledge of that truth

64.play: I put you o'er to heaven and to my mother:

65.play: Of that I doubt, as all men's children may.

66.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Out on thee, rude man! thou dost shame thy mother

67.play: And wound her honour with this diffidence.

68.play: BASTARD: I, madam? no, I have no reason for it;

69.play: That is my brother's plea and none of mine;

70.play: The which if he can prove, a' pops me out

71.play: At least from fair five hundred pound a year:

72.play: Heaven guard my mother's honour and my land!

73.play: KING JOHN: A good blunt fellow. Why, being younger born,

74.play: Doth he lay claim to thine inheritance?

75.play: BASTARD: I know not why, except to get the land.

76.play: But once he slander'd me with bastardy:

77.play: But whether I be as true begot or no,

78.play: That still I lay upon my mother's head,

79.play: But that I am as well begot, my liege,--

80.play: Fair fall the bones that took the pains for me!--

81.play: Compare our faces and be judge yourself.

82.play: If old sir Robert did beget us both

83.play: And were our father and this son like him,

84.play: O old sir Robert, father, on my knee

85.play: I give heaven thanks I was not like to thee!

86.play: KING JOHN: Why, what a madcap hath heaven lent us here!

87.play: QUEEN ELINOR: He hath a trick of Coeur-de-lion's face;

88.play: The accent of his tongue affecteth him.

89.play: Do you not read some tokens of my son

90.play: In the large composition of this man?

91.play: KING JOHN: Mine eye hath well examined his parts

92.play: And finds them perfect Richard. Sirrah, speak,

93.play: What doth move you to claim your brother's land?

94.play: BASTARD: Because he hath a half-face, like my father.

95.play: With half that face would he have all my land:

96.play: A half-faced groat five hundred pound a year!

97.play: ROBERT: My gracious liege, when that my father lived,

98.play: Your brother did employ my father much,--

99.play: BASTARD: Well, sir, by this you cannot get my land:

100.play: Your tale must be how he employ'd my mother.

101.play: ROBERT: And once dispatch'd him in an embassy

102.play: To Germany, there with the emperor

103.play: To treat of high affairs touching that time.

104.play: The advantage of his absence took the king

105.play: And in the mean time sojourn'd at my father's;

106.play: Where how he did prevail I shame to speak,

107.play: But truth is truth: large lengths of seas and shores

108.play: Between my father and my mother lay,

109.play: As I have heard my father speak himself,

110.play: When this same lusty gentleman was got.

111.play: Upon his death-bed he by will bequeath'd

112.play: His lands to me, and took it on his death

113.play: That this my mother's son was none of his;

114.play: And if he were, he came into the world

115.play: Full fourteen weeks before the course of time.

116.play: Then, good my liege, let me have what is mine,

117.play: My father's land, as was my father's will.

118.play: KING JOHN: Sirrah, your brother is legitimate;

119.play: Your father's wife did after wedlock bear him,

120.play: And if she did play false, the fault was hers;

121.play: Which fault lies on the hazards of all husbands

122.play: That marry wives. Tell me, how if my brother,

123.play: Who, as you say, took pains to get this son,

124.play: Had of your father claim'd this son for his?

125.play: In sooth, good friend, your father might have kept

126.play: This calf bred from his cow from all the world;

127.play: In sooth he might; then, if he were my brother's,

128.play: My brother might not claim him; nor your father,

129.play: Being none of his, refuse him: this concludes;

130.play: My mother's son did get your father's heir;

131.play: Your father's heir must have your father's land.

132.play: ROBERT: Shall then my father's will be of no force

133.play: To dispossess that child which is not his?

134.play: BASTARD: Of no more force to dispossess me, sir,

135.play: Than was his will to get me, as I think.

136.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Whether hadst thou rather be a Faulconbridge

137.play: And like thy brother, to enjoy thy land,

138.play: Or the reputed son of Coeur-de-lion,

139.play: Lord of thy presence and no land beside?

140.play: BASTARD: Madam, an if my brother had my shape,

141.play: And I had his, sir Robert's his, like him;

142.play: And if my legs were two such riding-rods,

143.play: My arms such eel-skins stuff'd, my face so thin

144.play: That in mine ear I durst not stick a rose

145.play: Lest men should say 'Look, where three-farthings goes!'

146.play: And, to his shape, were heir to all this land,

147.play: Would I might never stir from off this place,

148.play: I would give it every foot to have this face;

149.play: I would not be sir Nob in any case.

150.play: QUEEN ELINOR: I like thee well: wilt thou forsake thy fortune,

151.play: Bequeath thy land to him and follow me?

152.play: I am a soldier and now bound to France.

153.play: BASTARD: Brother, take you my land, I'll take my chance.

154.play: Your face hath got five hundred pound a year,

155.play: Yet sell your face for five pence and 'tis dear.

156.play: Madam, I'll follow you unto the death.

157.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Nay, I would have you go before me thither.

158.play: BASTARD: Our country manners give our betters way.

159.play: KING JOHN: What is thy name?

160.play: BASTARD: Philip, my liege, so is my name begun,

161.play: Philip, good old sir Robert's wife's eldest son.

162.play: KING JOHN: From henceforth bear his name whose form thou bear'st:

163.play: Kneel thou down Philip, but rise more great,

165.play: BASTARD: Brother by the mother's side, give me your hand:

166.play: My father gave me honour, yours gave land.

167.play: Now blessed by the hour, by night or day,

168.play: When I was got, sir Robert was away!

169.play: QUEEN ELINOR: The very spirit of Plantagenet!

170.play: I am thy grandam, Richard; call me so.

171.play: BASTARD: Madam, by chance but not by truth; what though?

172.play: Something about, a little from the right,

173.play: In at the window, or else o'er the hatch:

174.play: Who dares not stir by day must walk by night,

175.play: And have is have, however men do catch:

176.play: Near or far off, well won is still well shot,

177.play: And I am I, howe'er I was begot.

178.play: KING JOHN: Go, Faulconbridge: now hast thou thy desire;

179.play: A landless knight makes thee a landed squire.

180.play: Come, madam, and come, Richard, we must speed

181.play: For France, for France, for it is more than need.

182.play: BASTARD: Brother, adieu: good fortune come to thee!

183.play: For thou wast got i' the way of honesty.

184.play: A foot of honour better than I was;

185.play: But many a many foot of land the worse.

186.play: Well, now can I make any Joan a lady.

188.play: And if his name be George, I'll call him Peter;

189.play: For new-made honour doth forget men's names;

191.play: For your conversion. Now your traveller,

192.play: He and his toothpick at my worship's mess,

193.play: And when my knightly stomach is sufficed,

194.play: Why then I suck my teeth and catechise

195.play: My picked man of countries: 'My dear sir,'

196.play: Thus, leaning on mine elbow, I begin,

197.play: 'I shall beseech you'--that is question now;

198.play: And then comes answer like an Absey book:

199.play: 'O sir,' says answer, 'at your best command;

200.play: At your employment; at your service, sir;'

201.play: 'No, sir,' says question, 'I, sweet sir, at yours:'

202.play: And so, ere answer knows what question would,

204.play: And talking of the Alps and Apennines,

205.play: The Pyrenean and the river Po,

206.play: It draws toward supper in conclusion so.

208.play: And fits the mounting spirit like myself,

209.play: For he is but a bastard to the time

210.play: That doth not smack of observation;

211.play: And so am I, whether I smack or no;

212.play: And not alone in habit and device,

214.play: But from the inward motion to deliver

215.play: Sweet, sweet, sweet poison for the age's tooth:

216.play: Which, though I will not practise to deceive,

217.play: Yet, to avoid deceit, I mean to learn;

218.play: For it shall strew the footsteps of my rising.

219.play: But who comes in such haste in riding-robes?

220.play: What woman-post is this? hath she no husband

221.play: That will take pains to blow a horn before her?

222.play: O me! it is my mother. How now, good lady!

223.play: What brings you here to court so hastily?

224.play: LADY FAULCONBRIDGE: Where is that slave, thy brother? where is he,

225.play: That holds in chase mine honour up and down?

226.play: BASTARD: My brother Robert? old sir Robert's son?

227.play: Colbrand the giant, that same mighty man?

228.play: Is it sir Robert's son that you seek so?

229.play: LADY FAULCONBRIDGE: Sir Robert's son! Ay, thou unreverend boy,

230.play: Sir Robert's son: why scorn'st thou at sir Robert?

231.play: He is sir Robert's son, and so art thou.

232.play: BASTARD: James Gurney, wilt thou give us leave awhile?

235.play: There's toys abroad: anon I'll tell thee more.

236.play: Madam, I was not old sir Robert's son:

237.play: Sir Robert might have eat his part in me

238.play: Upon Good-Friday and ne'er broke his fast:

239.play: Sir Robert could do well: marry, to confess,

240.play: Could he get me? Sir Robert could not do it:

241.play: We know his handiwork: therefore, good mother,

242.play: To whom am I beholding for these limbs?

243.play: Sir Robert never holp to make this leg.

244.play: LADY FAULCONBRIDGE: Hast thou conspired with thy brother too,

245.play: That for thine own gain shouldst defend mine honour?

246.play: What means this scorn, thou most untoward knave?

247.play: BASTARD: Knight, knight, good mother, Basilisco-like.

248.play: What! I am dubb'd! I have it on my shoulder.

249.play: But, mother, I am not sir Robert's son;

250.play: I have disclaim'd sir Robert and my land;

251.play: Legitimation, name and all is gone:

252.play: Then, good my mother, let me know my father;

253.play: Some proper man, I hope: who was it, mother?

254.play: LADY FAULCONBRIDGE: Hast thou denied thyself a Faulconbridge?

255.play: BASTARD: As faithfully as I deny the devil.

256.play: LADY FAULCONBRIDGE: King Richard Coeur-de-lion was thy father:

257.play: By long and vehement suit I was seduced

258.play: To make room for him in my husband's bed:

259.play: Heaven lay not my transgression to my charge!

260.play: Thou art the issue of my dear offence,

261.play: Which was so strongly urged past my defence.

262.play: BASTARD: Now, by this light, were I to get again,

263.play: Madam, I would not wish a better father.

264.play: Some sins do bear their privilege on earth,

265.play: And so doth yours; your fault was not your folly:

266.play: Needs must you lay your heart at his dispose,

268.play: Against whose fury and unmatched force

269.play: The aweless lion could not wage the fight,

270.play: Nor keep his princely heart from Richard's hand.

271.play: He that perforce robs lions of their hearts

272.play: May easily win a woman's. Ay, my mother,

273.play: With all my heart I thank thee for my father!

274.play: Who lives and dares but say thou didst not well

275.play: When I was got, I'll send his soul to hell.

276.play: Come, lady, I will show thee to my kin;

277.play: And they shall say, when Richard me begot,

278.play: If thou hadst said him nay, it had been sin:

279.play: Who says it was, he lies; I say 'twas not.

1.play: Enter AUSTRIA and forces, drums, etc. on one side:  on the other KING PHILIP and his power; LEWIS, ARTHUR, CONSTANCE and attendants

2.play: LEWIS: Before Angiers well met, brave Austria.

3.play: Arthur, that great forerunner of thy blood,

4.play: Richard, that robb'd the lion of his heart

5.play: And fought the holy wars in Palestine,

6.play: By this brave duke came early to his grave:

7.play: And for amends to his posterity,

8.play: At our importance hither is he come,

9.play: To spread his colours, boy, in thy behalf,

11.play: Of thy unnatural uncle, English John:

12.play: Embrace him, love him, give him welcome hither.

13.play: ARTHUR: God shall forgive you Coeur-de-lion's death

14.play: The rather that you give his offspring life,

15.play: Shadowing their right under your wings of war:

16.play: I give you welcome with a powerless hand,

17.play: But with a heart full of unstained love:

18.play: Welcome before the gates of Angiers, duke.

19.play: LEWIS: A noble boy! Who would not do thee right?

20.play: AUSTRIA: Upon thy cheek lay I this zealous kiss,

21.play: As seal to this indenture of my love,

22.play: That to my home I will no more return,

23.play: Till Angiers and the right thou hast in France,

24.play: Together with that pale, that white-faced shore,

25.play: Whose foot spurns back the ocean's roaring tides

26.play: And coops from other lands her islanders,

27.play: Even till that England, hedged in with the main,

30.play: Even till that utmost corner of the west

31.play: Salute thee for her king: till then, fair boy,

32.play: Will I not think of home, but follow arms.

33.play: CONSTANCE: O, take his mother's thanks, a widow's thanks,

34.play: Till your strong hand shall help to give him strength

35.play: To make a more requital to your love!

36.play: AUSTRIA: The peace of heaven is theirs that lift their swords

37.play: In such a just and charitable war.

38.play: KING PHILIP: Well then, to work: our cannon shall be bent

39.play: Against the brows of this resisting town.

40.play: Call for our chiefest men of discipline,

41.play: To cull the plots of best advantages:

42.play: We'll lay before this town our royal bones,

43.play: Wade to the market-place in Frenchmen's blood,

44.play: But we will make it subject to this boy.

45.play: CONSTANCE: Stay for an answer to your embassy,

46.play: Lest unadvised you stain your swords with blood:

47.play: My Lord Chatillon may from England bring,

48.play: That right in peace which here we urge in war,

49.play: And then we shall repent each drop of blood

50.play: That hot rash haste so indirectly shed.

51.play: KING PHILIP: A wonder, lady! lo, upon thy wish,

53.play: What England says, say briefly, gentle lord;

54.play: We coldly pause for thee; Chatillon, speak.

55.play: CHATILLON: Then turn your forces from this paltry siege

56.play: And stir them up against a mightier task.

57.play: England, impatient of your just demands,

58.play: Hath put himself in arms: the adverse winds,

59.play: Whose leisure I have stay'd, have given him time

60.play: To land his legions all as soon as I;

61.play: His marches are expedient to this town,

62.play: His forces strong, his soldiers confident.

63.play: With him along is come the mother-queen,

64.play: An Ate, stirring him to blood and strife;

65.play: With her her niece, the Lady Blanch of Spain;

66.play: With them a bastard of the king's deceased,

67.play: And all the unsettled humours of the land,

69.play: With ladies' faces and fierce dragons' spleens,

70.play: Have sold their fortunes at their native homes,

71.play: Bearing their birthrights proudly on their backs,

72.play: To make hazard of new fortunes here:

73.play: In brief, a braver choice of dauntless spirits

74.play: Than now the English bottoms have waft o'er

75.play: Did nearer float upon the swelling tide,

76.play: To do offence and scath in Christendom.

77.play: The interruption of their churlish drums

78.play: Cuts off more circumstance: they are at hand,

79.play: To parley or to fight; therefore prepare.

80.play: KING PHILIP: How much unlook'd for is this expedition!

81.play: AUSTRIA: By how much unexpected, by so much

82.play: We must awake endavour for defence;

84.play: Let them be welcome then: we are prepared.

85.play: KING JOHN: Peace be to France, if France in peace permit

86.play: Our just and lineal entrance to our own;

87.play: If not, bleed France, and peace ascend to heaven,

88.play: Whiles we, God's wrathful agent, do correct

89.play: Their proud contempt that beats His peace to heaven.

90.play: KING PHILIP: Peace be to England, if that war return

91.play: From France to England, there to live in peace.

92.play: England we love; and for that England's sake

93.play: With burden of our armour here we sweat.

94.play: This toil of ours should be a work of thine;

95.play: But thou from loving England art so far,

96.play: That thou hast under-wrought his lawful king

97.play: Cut off the sequence of posterity,

98.play: Out-faced infant state and done a rape

99.play: Upon the maiden virtue of the crown.

100.play: Look here upon thy brother Geffrey's face;

101.play: These eyes, these brows, were moulded out of his:

102.play: This little abstract doth contain that large

103.play: Which died in Geffrey, and the hand of time

104.play: Shall draw this brief into as huge a volume.

105.play: That Geffrey was thy elder brother born,

106.play: And this his son; England was Geffrey's right

107.play: And this is Geffrey's: in the name of God

108.play: How comes it then that thou art call'd a king,

109.play: When living blood doth in these temples beat,

110.play: Which owe the crown that thou o'ermasterest?

111.play: KING JOHN: From whom hast thou this great commission, France,

112.play: To draw my answer from thy articles?

113.play: KING PHILIP: From that supernal judge, that stirs good thoughts

114.play: In any breast of strong authority,

115.play: To look into the blots and stains of right:

116.play: That judge hath made me guardian to this boy:

117.play: Under whose warrant I impeach thy wrong

118.play: And by whose help I mean to chastise it.

119.play: KING JOHN: Alack, thou dost usurp authority.

120.play: KING PHILIP: Excuse; it is to beat usurping down.

121.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Who is it thou dost call usurper, France?

122.play: CONSTANCE: Let me make answer; thy usurping son.

123.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Out, insolent! thy bastard shall be king,

124.play: That thou mayst be a queen, and cheque the world!

125.play: CONSTANCE: My bed was ever to thy son as true

126.play: As thine was to thy husband; and this boy

127.play: Liker in feature to his father Geffrey

128.play: Than thou and John in manners; being as like

129.play: As rain to water, or devil to his dam.

130.play: My boy a bastard! By my soul, I think

131.play: His father never was so true begot:

132.play: It cannot be, an if thou wert his mother.

133.play: QUEEN ELINOR: There's a good mother, boy, that blots thy father.

134.play: CONSTANCE: There's a good grandam, boy, that would blot thee.

137.play: AUSTRIA: What the devil art thou?

138.play: BASTARD: One that will play the devil, sir, with you,

139.play: An a' may catch your hide and you alone:

140.play: You are the hare of whom the proverb goes,

141.play: Whose valour plucks dead lions by the beard;

142.play: I'll smoke your skin-coat, an I catch you right;

143.play: Sirrah, look to't; i' faith, I will, i' faith.

144.play: BLANCH: O, well did he become that lion's robe

145.play: That did disrobe the lion of that robe!

146.play: BASTARD: It lies as sightly on the back of him

147.play: As great Alcides' shows upon an ass:

148.play: But, ass, I'll take that burthen from your back,

149.play: Or lay on that shall make your shoulders crack.

150.play: AUSTRIA: What craker is this same that deafs our ears

151.play: With this abundance of superfluous breath?

152.play: KING PHILIP: Lewis, determine what we shall do straight.

153.play: LEWIS: Women and fools, break off your conference.

154.play: King John, this is the very sum of all;

155.play: England and Ireland, Anjou, Touraine, Maine,

156.play: In right of Arthur do I claim of thee:

157.play: Wilt thou resign them and lay down thy arms?

158.play: KING JOHN: My life as soon: I do defy thee, France.

159.play: Arthur of Bretagne, yield thee to my hand;

160.play: And out of my dear love I'll give thee more

161.play: Than e'er the coward hand of France can win:

163.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Come to thy grandam, child.

164.play: CONSTANCE: Do, child, go to it grandam, child:

165.play: Give grandam kingdom, and it grandam will

166.play: Give it a plum, a cherry, and a fig:

169.play: I would that I were low laid in my grave:

170.play: I am not worth this coil that's made for me.

171.play: QUEEN ELINOR: His mother shames him so, poor boy, he weeps.

172.play: CONSTANCE: Now shame upon you, whether she does or no!

173.play: His grandam's wrongs, and not his mother's shames,

174.play: Draws those heaven-moving pearls from his poor eyes,

175.play: Which heaven shall take in nature of a fee;

176.play: Ay, with these crystal beads heaven shall be bribed

177.play: To do him justice and revenge on you.

178.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Thou monstrous slanderer of heaven and earth!

179.play: CONSTANCE: Thou monstrous injurer of heaven and earth!

180.play: Call not me slanderer; thou and thine usurp

182.play: Of this oppressed boy: this is thy eld'st son's son,

183.play: Infortunate in nothing but in thee:

184.play: Thy sins are visited in this poor child;

185.play: The canon of the law is laid on him,

189.play: CONSTANCE: I have but this to say,

190.play: That he is not only plagued for her sin,

191.play: But God hath made her sin and her the plague

192.play: On this removed issue, plague for her

193.play: And with her plague; her sin his injury,

194.play: Her injury the beadle to her sin,

195.play: All punish'd in the person of this child,

196.play: And all for her; a plague upon her!

197.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Thou unadvised scold, I can produce

198.play: A will that bars the title of thy son.

199.play: CONSTANCE: Ay, who doubts that? a will! a wicked will:

200.play: A woman's will; a canker'd grandam's will!

201.play: KING PHILIP: Peace, lady! pause, or be more temperate:

202.play: It ill beseems this presence to cry aim

210.play: KING PHILIP: You loving men of Angiers, Arthur's subjects,

211.play: Our trumpet call'd you to this gentle parle--

212.play: KING JOHN: For our advantage; therefore hear us first.

213.play: These flags of France, that are advanced here

214.play: Before the eye and prospect of your town,

215.play: Have hither march'd to your endamagement:

216.play: The cannons have their bowels full of wrath,

217.play: And ready mounted are they to spit forth

219.play: All preparation for a bloody siege

220.play: All merciless proceeding by these French

221.play: Confronts your city's eyes, your winking gates;

223.play: That as a waist doth girdle you about,

224.play: By the compulsion of their ordinance

226.play: Had been dishabited, and wide havoc made

227.play: For bloody power to rush upon your peace.

229.play: Who painfully with much expedient march

230.play: Have brought a countercheque before your gates,

231.play: To save unscratch'd your city's threatened cheeks,

232.play: Behold, the French amazed vouchsafe a parle;

233.play: And now, instead of bullets wrapp'd in fire,

234.play: To make a shaking fever in your walls,

235.play: They shoot but calm words folded up in smoke,

236.play: To make a faithless error in your ears:

238.play: And let us in, your king, whose labour'd spirits,

239.play: Forwearied in this action of swift speed,

240.play: Crave harbourage within your city walls.

241.play: KING PHILIP: When I have said, make answer to us both.

242.play: Lo, in this right hand, whose protection

243.play: Is most divinely vow'd upon the right

244.play: Of him it holds, stands young Plantagenet,

245.play: Son to the elder brother of this man,

246.play: And king o'er him and all that he enjoys:

247.play: For this down-trodden equity, we tread

248.play: In warlike march these greens before your town,

249.play: Being no further enemy to you

250.play: Than the constraint of hospitable zeal

251.play: In the relief of this oppressed child

253.play: To pay that duty which you truly owe

254.play: To that owes it, namely this young prince:

255.play: And then our arms, like to a muzzled bear,

256.play: Save in aspect, hath all offence seal'd up;

257.play: Our cannons' malice vainly shall be spent

258.play: Against the invulnerable clouds of heaven;

259.play: And with a blessed and unvex'd retire,

260.play: With unhack'd swords and helmets all unbruised,

261.play: We will bear home that lusty blood again

264.play: But if you fondly pass our proffer'd offer,

267.play: Though all these English and their discipline

275.play: And if not that, I bring you witnesses,

291.play: At your den, sirrah, with your lioness

302.play: Re-enter KING JOHN and KING PHILIP, with their powers, severally

303.play: KING JOHN: France, hast thou yet more blood to cast away?

304.play: Say, shall the current of our right run on?

305.play: Whose passage, vex'd with thy impediment,

311.play: In this hot trial, more than we of France;

312.play: Rather, lost more. And by this hand I swear,

313.play: That sways the earth this climate overlooks,

317.play: Gracing the scroll that tells of this war's loss

319.play: BASTARD: Ha, majesty! how high thy glory towers,

320.play: When the rich blood of kings is set on fire!

321.play: O, now doth Death line his dead chaps with steel;

322.play: The swords of soldiers are his teeth, his fangs;

323.play: And now he feasts, mousing the flesh of men,

325.play: Why stand these royal fronts amazed thus?

326.play: Cry, 'havoc!' kings; back to the stained field,

327.play: You equal potents, fiery kindled spirits!

328.play: Then let confusion of one part confirm

329.play: The other's peace: till then, blows, blood and death!

330.play: KING JOHN: Whose party do the townsmen yet admit?

331.play: KING PHILIP: Speak, citizens, for England; who's your king?

332.play: Know him in us, that here hold up his right.

333.play: KING JOHN: In us, that are our own great deputy

334.play: And bear possession of our person here,

335.play: Lord of our presence, Angiers, and of you.

336.play: BASTARD: By heaven, these scroyles of Angiers flout you, kings,

337.play: And stand securely on their battlements,

338.play: As in a theatre, whence they gape and point

339.play: At your industrious scenes and acts of death.

340.play: Your royal presences be ruled by me:

341.play: Do like the mutines of Jerusalem,

342.play: Be friends awhile and both conjointly bend

343.play: Your sharpest deeds of malice on this town:

344.play: By east and west let France and England mount

345.play: Their battering cannon charged to the mouths,

346.play: Till their soul-fearing clamours have brawl'd down

347.play: The flinty ribs of this contemptuous city:

348.play: I'ld play incessantly upon these jades,

350.play: Leave them as naked as the vulgar air.

351.play: That done, dissever your united strengths,

352.play: And part your mingled colours once again;

353.play: Turn face to face and bloody point to point;

354.play: Then, in a moment, Fortune shall cull forth

355.play: Out of one side her happy minion,

356.play: To whom in favour she shall give the day,

357.play: And kiss him with a glorious victory.

358.play: How like you this wild counsel, mighty states?

359.play: Smacks it not something of the policy?

360.play: KING JOHN: Now, by the sky that hangs above our heads,

361.play: I like it well. France, shall we knit our powers

362.play: And lay this Angiers even to the ground;

363.play: Then after fight who shall be king of it?

364.play: BASTARD: An if thou hast the mettle of a king,

365.play: Being wronged as we are by this peevish town,

366.play: Turn thou the mouth of thy artillery,

367.play: As we will ours, against these saucy walls;

368.play: And when that we have dash'd them to the ground,

369.play: Why then defy each other and pell-mell

370.play: Make work upon ourselves, for heaven or hell.

371.play: KING PHILIP: Let it be so. Say, where will you assault?

372.play: KING JOHN: We from the west will send destruction

375.play: KING PHILIP: Our thunder from the south

376.play: Shall rain their drift of bullets on this town.

377.play: BASTARD: O prudent discipline! From north to south:

378.play: Austria and France shoot in each other's mouth:

379.play: I'll stir them to it. Come, away, away!

380.play: KING JOHN: Speak on with favour; we are bent to hear.

382.play: That shakes the rotten carcass of old Death

383.play: Out of his rags! Here's a large mouth, indeed,

384.play: That spits forth death and mountains, rocks and seas,

385.play: Talks as familiarly of roaring lions

386.play: As maids of thirteen do of puppy-dogs!

387.play: What cannoneer begot this lusty blood?

388.play: He speaks plain cannon fire, and smoke and bounce;

389.play: He gives the bastinado with his tongue:

390.play: Our ears are cudgell'd; not a word of his

391.play: But buffets better than a fist of France:

392.play: Zounds! I was never so bethump'd with words

393.play: Since I first call'd my brother's father dad.

394.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Son, list to this conjunction, make this match;

395.play: Give with our niece a dowry large enough:

396.play: For by this knot thou shalt so surely tie

397.play: Thy now unsured assurance to the crown,

398.play: That yon green boy shall have no sun to ripe

399.play: The bloom that promiseth a mighty fruit.

400.play: I see a yielding in the looks of France;

401.play: Mark, how they whisper: urge them while their souls

403.play: Lest zeal, now melted by the windy breath

404.play: Of soft petitions, pity and remorse,

405.play: Cool and congeal again to what it was.

407.play: To speak unto this city: what say you?

408.play: KING JOHN: If that the Dauphin there, thy princely son,

409.play: Can in this book of beauty read 'I love,'

410.play: Her dowry shall weigh equal with a queen:

411.play: For Anjou and fair Touraine, Maine, Poictiers,

412.play: And all that we upon this side the sea,

413.play: Except this city now by us besieged,

414.play: Find liable to our crown and dignity,

421.play: A wonder, or a wondrous miracle,

422.play: The shadow of myself form'd in her eye:

423.play: Which being but the shadow of your son,

424.play: Becomes a sun and makes your son a shadow:

425.play: I do protest I never loved myself

426.play: Till now infixed I beheld myself

427.play: Drawn in the flattering table of her eye.

428.play: BASTARD: Drawn in the flattering table of her eye!

429.play: Hang'd in the frowning wrinkle of her brow!

430.play: And quarter'd in her heart! he doth espy

431.play: Himself love's traitor: this is pity now,

432.play: That hang'd and drawn and quartered, there should be

433.play: In such a love so vile a lout as he.

435.play: If he see aught in you that makes him like,

436.play: That any thing he sees, which moves his liking,

437.play: I can with ease translate it to my will;

438.play: Or if you will, to speak more properly,

439.play: I will enforce it easily to my love.

440.play: Further I will not flatter you, my lord,

441.play: That all I see in you is worthy love,

442.play: Than this; that nothing do I see in you,

446.play: BLANCH: That she is bound in honour still to do

447.play: What you in wisdom still vouchsafe to say.

449.play: LEWIS: Nay, ask me if I can refrain from love;

450.play: For I do love her most unfeignedly.

463.play: The rites of marriage shall be solemnized.

464.play: Is not the Lady Constance in this troop?

465.play: I know she is not, for this match made up

466.play: Her presence would have interrupted much:

467.play: Where is she and her son? tell me, who knows.

470.play: Will give her sadness very little cure.

471.play: Brother of England, how may we content

472.play: This widow lady? In her right we came;

473.play: Which we, God knows, have turn'd another way,

475.play: KING JOHN: We will heal up all;

476.play: For we'll create young Arthur Duke of Bretagne

477.play: And Earl of Richmond; and this rich fair town

478.play: We make him lord of. Call the Lady Constance;

479.play: Some speedy messenger bid her repair

480.play: To our solemnity: I trust we shall,

481.play: If not fill up the measure of her will,

482.play: Yet in some measure satisfy her so

483.play: That we shall stop her exclamation.

484.play: Go we, as well as haste will suffer us,

485.play: To this unlook'd for, unprepared pomp.

486.play: BASTARD: Mad world! mad kings! mad composition!

487.play: John, to stop Arthur's title in the whole,

488.play: Hath willingly departed with a part,

489.play: And France, whose armour conscience buckled on,

490.play: Whom zeal and charity brought to the field

491.play: As God's own soldier, rounded in the ear

492.play: With that same purpose-changer, that sly devil,

493.play: That broker, that still breaks the pate of faith,

494.play: That daily break-vow, he that wins of all,

495.play: Of kings, of beggars, old men, young men, maids,

496.play: Who, having no external thing to lose

497.play: But the word 'maid,' cheats the poor maid of that,

499.play: Commodity, the bias of the world,

500.play: The world, who of itself is peised well,

501.play: Made to run even upon even ground,

502.play: Till this advantage, this vile-drawing bias,

503.play: This sway of motion, this Commodity,

504.play: Makes it take head from all indifferency,

505.play: From all direction, purpose, course, intent:

506.play: And this same bias, this Commodity,

507.play: This bawd, this broker, this all-changing word,

508.play: Clapp'd on the outward eye of fickle France,

509.play: Hath drawn him from his own determined aid,

510.play: From a resolved and honourable war,

511.play: To a most base and vile-concluded peace.

512.play: And why rail I on this Commodity?

513.play: But for because he hath not woo'd me yet:

514.play: Not that I have the power to clutch my hand,

515.play: When his fair angels would salute my palm;

516.play: But for my hand, as unattempted yet,

517.play: Like a poor beggar, raileth on the rich.

518.play: Well, whiles I am a beggar, I will rail

519.play: And say there is no sin but to be rich;

520.play: And being rich, my virtue then shall be

521.play: To say there is no vice but beggary.

522.play: Since kings break faith upon commodity,

523.play: Gain, be my lord, for I will worship thee.

2.play: CONSTANCE: Gone to be married! gone to swear a peace!

3.play: False blood to false blood join'd! gone to be friends!

4.play: Shall Lewis have Blanch, and Blanch those provinces?

5.play: It is not so; thou hast misspoke, misheard:

6.play: Be well advised, tell o'er thy tale again:

7.play: It cannot be; thou dost but say 'tis so:

8.play: I trust I may not trust thee; for thy word

9.play: Is but the vain breath of a common man:

10.play: Believe me, I do not believe thee, man;

11.play: I have a king's oath to the contrary.

12.play: Thou shalt be punish'd for thus frighting me,

13.play: For I am sick and capable of fears,

14.play: Oppress'd with wrongs and therefore full of fears,

15.play: A widow, husbandless, subject to fears,

16.play: A woman, naturally born to fears;

17.play: And though thou now confess thou didst but jest,

18.play: With my vex'd spirits I cannot take a truce,

19.play: But they will quake and tremble all this day.

20.play: What dost thou mean by shaking of thy head?

21.play: Why dost thou look so sadly on my son?

22.play: What means that hand upon that breast of thine?

23.play: Why holds thine eye that lamentable rheum,

24.play: Like a proud river peering o'er his bounds?

25.play: Be these sad signs confirmers of thy words?

26.play: Then speak again; not all thy former tale,

27.play: But this one word, whether thy tale be true.

28.play: SALISBURY: As true as I believe you think them false

29.play: That give you cause to prove my saying true.

30.play: CONSTANCE: O, if thou teach me to believe this sorrow,

31.play: Teach thou this sorrow how to make me die,

32.play: And let belief and life encounter so

33.play: As doth the fury of two desperate men

36.play: France friend with England, what becomes of me?

37.play: Fellow, be gone: I cannot brook thy sight:

40.play: But spoke the harm that is by others done?

44.play: CONSTANCE: If thou, that bid'st me be content, wert grim,

45.play: Ugly and slanderous to thy mother's womb,

46.play: Full of unpleasing blots and sightless stains,

50.play: For then I should not love thee, no, nor thou

51.play: Become thy great birth nor deserve a crown.

52.play: But thou art fair, and at thy birth, dear boy,

53.play: Nature and Fortune join'd to make thee great:

54.play: Of Nature's gifts thou mayst with lilies boast,

62.play: That strumpet Fortune, that usurping John!

63.play: Tell me, thou fellow, is not France forsworn?

64.play: Envenom him with words, or get thee gone

65.play: And leave those woes alone which I alone

68.play: I may not go without you to the kings.

69.play: CONSTANCE: Thou mayst, thou shalt; I will not go with thee:

70.play: I will instruct my sorrows to be proud;

71.play: For grief is proud and makes his owner stoop.

72.play: To me and to the state of my great grief

73.play: Let kings assemble; for my grief's so great

74.play: That no supporter but the huge firm earth

75.play: Can hold it up: here I and sorrows sit;

76.play: Here is my throne, bid kings come bow to it.

77.play: KING PHILIP: 'Tis true, fair daughter; and this blessed day

78.play: Ever in France shall be kept festival:

79.play: To solemnize this day the glorious sun

80.play: Stays in his course and plays the alchemist,

81.play: Turning with splendor of his precious eye

82.play: The meagre cloddy earth to glittering gold:

83.play: The yearly course that brings this day about

84.play: Shall never see it but a holiday.

85.play: CONSTANCE: A wicked day, and not a holy day!

86.play: What hath this day deserved? what hath it done,

87.play: That it in golden letters should be set

88.play: Among the high tides in the calendar?

89.play: Nay, rather turn this day out of the week,

90.play: This day of shame, oppression, perjury.

91.play: Or, if it must stand still, let wives with child

92.play: Pray that their burthens may not fall this day,

94.play: But on this day let seamen fear no wreck;

100.play: Have I not pawn'd to you my majesty?

152.play: Thou canst not, cardinal, devise a name

156.play: Add thus much more, that no Italian priest

157.play: Shall tithe or toll in our dominions;

175.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: Then, by the lawful power that I have,

176.play: Thou shalt stand cursed and excommunicate.

177.play: And blessed shall he be that doth revolt

178.play: From his allegiance to an heretic;

179.play: And meritorious shall that hand be call'd,

180.play: Canonized and worshipped as a saint,

181.play: That takes away by any secret course

183.play: CONSTANCE: O, lawful let it be

184.play: That I have room with Rome to curse awhile!

185.play: Good father cardinal, cry thou amen

186.play: To my keen curses; for without my wrong

187.play: There is no tongue hath power to curse him right.

188.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: There's law and warrant, lady, for my curse.

189.play: CONSTANCE: And for mine too: when law can do no right,

190.play: Let it be lawful that law bar no wrong:

191.play: Law cannot give my child his kingdom here,

192.play: For he that holds his kingdom holds the law;

193.play: Therefore, since law itself is perfect wrong,

194.play: How can the law forbid my tongue to curse?

195.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: Philip of France, on peril of a curse,

196.play: Let go the hand of that arch-heretic;

197.play: And raise the power of France upon his head,

198.play: Unless he do submit himself to Rome.

199.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Look'st thou pale, France? do not let go thy hand.

200.play: CONSTANCE: Look to that, devil; lest that France repent,

201.play: And by disjoining hands, hell lose a soul.

202.play: AUSTRIA: King Philip, listen to the cardinal.

203.play: BASTARD: And hang a calf's-skin on his recreant limbs.

204.play: AUSTRIA: Well, ruffian, I must pocket up these wrongs, Because--

205.play: BASTARD: Your breeches best may carry them.

206.play: KING JOHN: Philip, what say'st thou to the cardinal?

207.play: CONSTANCE: What should he say, but as the cardinal?

208.play: LEWIS: Bethink you, father; for the difference

209.play: Is purchase of a heavy curse from Rome,

210.play: Or the light loss of England for a friend:

212.play: BLANCH: That's the curse of Rome.

213.play: CONSTANCE: O Lewis, stand fast! the devil tempts thee here

214.play: In likeness of a new untrimmed bride.

215.play: BLANCH: The Lady Constance speaks not from her faith,

217.play: CONSTANCE: O, if thou grant my need,

218.play: Which only lives but by the death of faith,

219.play: That need must needs infer this principle,

220.play: That faith would live again by death of need.

221.play: O then, tread down my need, and faith mounts up;

222.play: Keep my need up, and faith is trodden down!

223.play: KING JOHN: The king is moved, and answers not to this.

224.play: CONSTANCE: O, be removed from him, and answer well!

225.play: AUSTRIA: Do so, King Philip; hang no more in doubt.

226.play: BASTARD: Hang nothing but a calf's-skin, most sweet lout.

227.play: KING PHILIP: I am perplex'd, and know not what to say.

228.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: What canst thou say but will perplex thee more,

229.play: If thou stand excommunicate and cursed?

230.play: KING PHILIP: Good reverend father, make my person yours,

231.play: And tell me how you would bestow yourself.

232.play: This royal hand and mine are newly knit,

233.play: And the conjunction of our inward souls

234.play: Married in league, coupled and linked together

235.play: With all religious strength of sacred vows;

236.play: The latest breath that gave the sound of words

237.play: Was deep-sworn faith, peace, amity, true love

238.play: Between our kingdoms and our royal selves,

239.play: And even before this truce, but new before,

240.play: No longer than we well could wash our hands

241.play: To clap this royal bargain up of peace,

242.play: Heaven knows, they were besmear'd and over-stain'd

243.play: With slaughter's pencil, where revenge did paint

244.play: The fearful difference of incensed kings:

245.play: And shall these hands, so lately purged of blood,

246.play: So newly join'd in love, so strong in both,

247.play: Unyoke this seizure and this kind regreet?

248.play: Play fast and loose with faith? so jest with heaven,

249.play: Make such unconstant children of ourselves,

250.play: As now again to snatch our palm from palm,

251.play: Unswear faith sworn, and on the marriage-bed

252.play: Of smiling peace to march a bloody host,

253.play: And make a riot on the gentle brow

254.play: Of true sincerity? O, holy sir,

255.play: My reverend father, let it not be so!

256.play: Out of your grace, devise, ordain, impose

257.play: Some gentle order; and then we shall be blest

258.play: To do your pleasure and continue friends.

259.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: All form is formless, order orderless,

260.play: Save what is opposite to England's love.

261.play: Therefore to arms! be champion of our church,

262.play: Or let the church, our mother, breathe her curse,

263.play: A mother's curse, on her revolting son.

264.play: France, thou mayst hold a serpent by the tongue,

265.play: A chafed lion by the mortal paw,

266.play: A fasting tiger safer by the tooth,

267.play: Than keep in peace that hand which thou dost hold.

268.play: KING PHILIP: I may disjoin my hand, but not my faith.

269.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: So makest thou faith an enemy to faith;

270.play: And like a civil war set'st oath to oath,

271.play: Thy tongue against thy tongue. O, let thy vow

272.play: First made to heaven, first be to heaven perform'd,

273.play: That is, to be the champion of our church!

274.play: What since thou sworest is sworn against thyself

275.play: And may not be performed by thyself,

276.play: For that which thou hast sworn to do amiss

277.play: Is not amiss when it is truly done,

278.play: And being not done, where doing tends to ill,

279.play: The truth is then most done not doing it:

280.play: The better act of purposes mistook

281.play: Is to mistake again; though indirect,

283.play: And falsehood falsehood cures, as fire cools fire

284.play: Within the scorched veins of one new-burn'd.

285.play: It is religion that doth make vows kept;

286.play: But thou hast sworn against religion,

287.play: By what thou swear'st against the thing thou swear'st,

288.play: And makest an oath the surety for thy truth

289.play: Against an oath: the truth thou art unsure

290.play: To swear, swears only not to be forsworn;

291.play: Else what a mockery should it be to swear!

292.play: But thou dost swear only to be forsworn;

293.play: And most forsworn, to keep what thou dost swear.

294.play: Therefore thy later vows against thy first

295.play: Is in thyself rebellion to thyself;

296.play: And better conquest never canst thou make

297.play: Than arm thy constant and thy nobler parts

299.play: Upon which better part our prayers come in,

300.play: If thou vouchsafe them. But if not, then know

301.play: The peril of our curses light on thee

302.play: So heavy as thou shalt not shake them off,

303.play: But in despair die under their black weight.

306.play: Will not a calfs-skin stop that mouth of thine?

309.play: Against the blood that thou hast married?

310.play: What, shall our feast be kept with slaughter'd men?

311.play: Shall braying trumpets and loud churlish drums,

312.play: Clamours of hell, be measures to our pomp?

313.play: O husband, hear me! ay, alack, how new

314.play: Is husband in my mouth! even for that name,

315.play: Which till this time my tongue did ne'er pronounce,

316.play: Upon my knee I beg, go not to arms

319.play: Made hard with kneeling, I do pray to thee,

320.play: Thou virtuous Dauphin, alter not the doom

322.play: BLANCH: Now shall I see thy love: what motive may

323.play: Be stronger with thee than the name of wife?

324.play: CONSTANCE: That which upholdeth him that thee upholds,

325.play: His honour: O, thine honour, Lewis, thine honour!

326.play: LEWIS: I muse your majesty doth seem so cold,

327.play: When such profound respects do pull you on.

328.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: I will denounce a curse upon his head.

329.play: KING PHILIP: Thou shalt not need. England, I will fall from thee.

330.play: CONSTANCE: O fair return of banish'd majesty!

331.play: QUEEN ELINOR: O foul revolt of French inconstancy!

332.play: KING JOHN: France, thou shalt rue this hour within this hour.

333.play: BASTARD: Old Time the clock-setter, that bald sexton Time,

334.play: Is it as he will? well then, France shall rue.

335.play: BLANCH: The sun's o'ercast with blood: fair day, adieu!

336.play: Which is the side that I must go withal?

337.play: I am with both: each army hath a hand;

338.play: And in their rage, I having hold of both,

339.play: They swirl asunder and dismember me.

340.play: Husband, I cannot pray that thou mayst win;

341.play: Uncle, I needs must pray that thou mayst lose;

342.play: Father, I may not wish the fortune thine;

343.play: Grandam, I will not wish thy fortunes thrive:

344.play: Whoever wins, on that side shall I lose

345.play: Assured loss before the match be play'd.

346.play: LEWIS: Lady, with me, with me thy fortune lies.

347.play: BLANCH: There where my fortune lives, there my life dies.

348.play: KING JOHN: Cousin, go draw our puissance together.

349.play: France, I am burn'd up with inflaming wrath;

350.play: A rage whose heat hath this condition,

351.play: That nothing can allay, nothing but blood,

352.play: The blood, and dearest-valued blood, of France.

353.play: KING PHILIP: Thy rage sham burn thee up, and thou shalt turn

354.play: To ashes, ere our blood shall quench that fire:

355.play: Look to thyself, thou art in jeopardy.

356.play: KING JOHN: No more than he that threats. To arms let's hie!

41.play: Is all too wanton and too full of gawds

9.play: And bloody England into England gone,

11.play: LEWIS: What he hath won, that hath he fortified:

12.play: So hot a speed with such advice disposed,

13.play: Such temperate order in so fierce a cause,

14.play: Doth want example: who hath read or heard

15.play: Of any kindred action like to this?

16.play: KING PHILIP: Well could I bear that England had this praise,

17.play: So we could find some pattern of our shame.

18.play: Look, who comes here! a grave unto a soul;

19.play: Holding the eternal spirit against her will,

21.play: I prithee, lady, go away with me.

22.play: CONSTANCE: Lo, now I now see the issue of your peace.

23.play: KING PHILIP: Patience, good lady! comfort, gentle Constance!

24.play: CONSTANCE: No, I defy all counsel, all redress,

25.play: But that which ends all counsel, true redress,

26.play: Death, death; O amiable lovely death!

28.play: Arise forth from the couch of lasting night,

29.play: Thou hate and terror to prosperity,

30.play: And I will kiss thy detestable bones

31.play: And put my eyeballs in thy vaulty brows

32.play: And ring these fingers with thy household worms

33.play: And stop this gap of breath with fulsome dust

34.play: And be a carrion monster like thyself:

35.play: Come, grin on me, and I will think thou smilest

36.play: And buss thee as thy wife. Misery's love,

38.play: KING PHILIP: O fair affliction, peace!

39.play: CONSTANCE: No, no, I will not, having breath to cry:

40.play: O, that my tongue were in the thunder's mouth!

41.play: Then with a passion would I shake the world;

42.play: And rouse from sleep that fell anatomy

43.play: Which cannot hear a lady's feeble voice,

45.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: Lady, you utter madness, and not sorrow.

46.play: CONSTANCE: Thou art not holy to belie me so;

47.play: I am not mad: this hair I tear is mine;

48.play: My name is Constance; I was Geffrey's wife;

49.play: Young Arthur is my son, and he is lost:

50.play: I am not mad: I would to heaven I were!

51.play: For then, 'tis like I should forget myself:

52.play: O, if I could, what grief should I forget!

53.play: Preach some philosophy to make me mad,

54.play: And thou shalt be canonized, cardinal;

55.play: For being not mad but sensible of grief,

57.play: How I may be deliver'd of these woes,

58.play: And teaches me to kill or hang myself:

59.play: If I were mad, I should forget my son,

60.play: Or madly think a babe of clouts were he:

61.play: I am not mad; too well, too well I feel

63.play: KING PHILIP: Bind up those tresses. O, what love I note

64.play: In the fair multitude of those her hairs!

65.play: Where but by chance a silver drop hath fallen,

66.play: Even to that drop ten thousand wiry friends

67.play: Do glue themselves in sociable grief,

70.play: CONSTANCE: To England, if you will.

71.play: KING PHILIP: Bind up your hairs.

72.play: CONSTANCE: Yes, that I will; and wherefore will I do it?

73.play: I tore them from their bonds and cried aloud

74.play: 'O that these hands could so redeem my son,

75.play: As they have given these hairs their liberty!'

76.play: But now I envy at their liberty,

77.play: And will again commit them to their bonds,

78.play: Because my poor child is a prisoner.

79.play: And, father cardinal, I have heard you say

80.play: That we shall see and know our friends in heaven:

81.play: If that be true, I shall see my boy again;

82.play: For since the birth of Cain, the first male child,

85.play: But now will canker-sorrow eat my bud

86.play: And chase the native beauty from his cheek

87.play: And he will look as hollow as a ghost,

89.play: And so he'll die; and, rising so again,

134.play: And if thou want'st a cord, the smallest thread

135.play: That ever spider twisted from her womb

136.play: Will serve to strangle thee, a rush will be a beam

137.play: To hang thee on; or wouldst thou drown thyself,

138.play: Put but a little water in a spoon,

139.play: And it shall be as all the ocean,

140.play: Enough to stifle such a villain up.

141.play: I do suspect thee very grievously.

142.play: HUBERT: If I in act, consent, or sin of thought,

143.play: Be guilty of the stealing that sweet breath

144.play: Which was embounded in this beauteous clay,

145.play: Let hell want pains enough to torture me.

147.play: BASTARD: Go, bear him in thine arms.

148.play: I am amazed, methinks, and lose my way

149.play: Among the thorns and dangers of this world.

150.play: How easy dost thou take all England up!

151.play: From forth this morsel of dead royalty,

152.play: The life, the right and truth of all this realm

153.play: Is fled to heaven; and England now is left

154.play: To tug and scamble and to part by the teeth

155.play: The unowed interest of proud-swelling state.

156.play: Now for the bare-pick'd bone of majesty

157.play: Doth dogged war bristle his angry crest

158.play: And snarleth in the gentle eyes of peace:

159.play: Now powers from home and discontents at home

160.play: Meet in one line; and vast confusion waits,

161.play: As doth a raven on a sick-fall'n beast,

162.play: The imminent decay of wrested pomp.

163.play: Now happy he whose cloak and cincture can

164.play: Hold out this tempest. Bear away that child

165.play: And follow me with speed: I'll to the king:

166.play: A thousand businesses are brief in hand,

167.play: And heaven itself doth frown upon the land.

11.play: A voluntary zeal and an unurged faith

12.play: To your proceedings; yet believe me, prince,

13.play: I am not glad that such a sore of time

15.play: And heal the inveterate canker of one wound

16.play: By making many. O, it grieves my soul,

18.play: To be a widow-maker! O, and there

20.play: Cries out upon the name of Salisbury!

21.play: But such is the infection of the time,

23.play: We cannot deal but with the very hand

24.play: Of stern injustice and confused wrong.

25.play: And is't not pity, O my grieved friends,

26.play: That we, the sons and children of this isle,

27.play: Were born to see so sad an hour as this;

28.play: Wherein we step after a stranger march

29.play: Upon her gentle bosom, and fill up

30.play: Her enemies' ranks,--I must withdraw and weep

31.play: Upon the spot of this enforced cause,--

32.play: To grace the gentry of a land remote,

34.play: What, here? O nation, that thou couldst remove!

35.play: That Neptune's arms, who clippeth thee about,

36.play: Would bear thee from the knowledge of thyself,

37.play: And grapple thee unto a pagan shore;

38.play: Where these two Christian armies might combine

39.play: The blood of malice in a vein of league,

40.play: And not to spend it so unneighbourly!

41.play: LEWIS: A noble temper dost thou show in this;

42.play: And great affections wrestling in thy bosom

43.play: Doth make an earthquake of nobility.

44.play: O, what a noble combat hast thou fought

45.play: Between compulsion and a brave respect!

46.play: Let me wipe off this honourable dew,

47.play: That silverly doth progress on thy cheeks:

48.play: My heart hath melted at a lady's tears,

50.play: But this effusion of such manly drops,

51.play: This shower, blown up by tempest of the soul,

52.play: Startles mine eyes, and makes me more amazed

53.play: Than had I seen the vaulty top of heaven

54.play: Figured quite o'er with burning meteors.

55.play: Lift up thy brow, renowned Salisbury,

56.play: And with a great heart heave away the storm:

57.play: Commend these waters to those baby eyes

59.play: Nor met with fortune other than at feasts,

60.play: Full of warm blood, of mirth, of gossiping.

61.play: Come, come; for thou shalt thrust thy hand as deep

62.play: Into the purse of rich prosperity

65.play: And even there, methinks, an angel spake:

66.play: Look, where the holy legate comes apace,

67.play: To give us warrant from the hand of heaven

68.play: And on our actions set the name of right

70.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: Hail, noble prince of France!

71.play: The next is this, King John hath reconciled

72.play: Himself to Rome; his spirit is come in,

73.play: That so stood out against the holy church,

74.play: The great metropolis and see of Rome:

75.play: Therefore thy threatening colours now wind up;

76.play: And tame the savage spirit of wild war,

77.play: That like a lion foster'd up at hand,

78.play: It may lie gently at the foot of peace,

79.play: And be no further harmful than in show.

80.play: LEWIS: Your grace shall pardon me, I will not back:

81.play: I am too high-born to be propertied,

82.play: To be a secondary at control,

84.play: To any sovereign state throughout the world.

85.play: Your breath first kindled the dead coal of wars

86.play: Between this chastised kingdom and myself,

87.play: And brought in matter that should feed this fire;

88.play: And now 'tis far too huge to be blown out

89.play: With that same weak wind which enkindled it.

90.play: You taught me how to know the face of right,

91.play: Acquainted me with interest to this land,

92.play: Yea, thrust this enterprise into my heart;

93.play: And come ye now to tell me John hath made

94.play: His peace with Rome? What is that peace to me?

95.play: I, by the honour of my marriage-bed,

96.play: After young Arthur, claim this land for mine;

97.play: And, now it is half-conquer'd, must I back

98.play: Because that John hath made his peace with Rome?

99.play: Am I Rome's slave? What penny hath Rome borne,

100.play: What men provided, what munition sent,

101.play: To underprop this action? Is't not I

102.play: That undergo this charge? who else but I,

103.play: And such as to my claim are liable,

104.play: Sweat in this business and maintain this war?

105.play: Have I not heard these islanders shout out

106.play: 'Vive le roi!' as I have bank'd their towns?

107.play: Have I not here the best cards for the game,

108.play: To win this easy match play'd for a crown?

109.play: And shall I now give o'er the yielded set?

110.play: No, no, on my soul, it never shall be said.

111.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: You look but on the outside of this work.

112.play: LEWIS: Outside or inside, I will not return

113.play: Till my attempt so much be glorified

114.play: As to my ample hope was promised

115.play: Before I drew this gallant head of war,

116.play: And cull'd these fiery spirits from the world,

117.play: To outlook conquest and to win renown

118.play: Even in the jaws of danger and of death.

119.play: What lusty trumpet thus doth summon us?

120.play: BASTARD: According to the fair play of the world,

121.play: Let me have audience; I am sent to speak:

122.play: My holy lord of Milan, from the king

123.play: I come, to learn how you have dealt for him;

124.play: And, as you answer, I do know the scope

125.play: And warrant limited unto my tongue.

126.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: The Dauphin is too wilful-opposite,

127.play: And will not temporize with my entreaties;

128.play: He flatly says he'll not lay down his arms.

129.play: BASTARD: By all the blood that ever fury breathed,

130.play: The youth says well. Now hear our English king;

131.play: For thus his royalty doth speak in me.

132.play: He is prepared, and reason too he should:

134.play: This harness'd masque and unadvised revel,

135.play: This unhair'd sauciness and boyish troops,

136.play: The king doth smile at; and is well prepared

137.play: To whip this dwarfish war, these pigmy arms,

138.play: From out the circle of his territories.

139.play: That hand which had the strength, even at your door,

140.play: To cudgel you and make you take the hatch,

141.play: To dive like buckets in concealed wells,

142.play: To crouch in litter of your stable planks,

143.play: To lie like pawns lock'd up in chests and trunks,

144.play: To hug with swine, to seek sweet safety out

145.play: In vaults and prisons, and to thrill and shake

146.play: Even at the crying of your nation's crow,

147.play: Thinking his voice an armed Englishman;

148.play: Shall that victorious hand be feebled here,

149.play: That in your chambers gave you chastisement?

150.play: No: know the gallant monarch is in arms

151.play: And like an eagle o'er his aery towers,

152.play: To souse annoyance that comes near his nest.

153.play: And you degenerate, you ingrate revolts,

154.play: You bloody Neroes, ripping up the womb

155.play: Of your dear mother England, blush for shame;

156.play: For your own ladies and pale-visaged maids

157.play: Like Amazons come tripping after drums,

158.play: Their thimbles into armed gauntlets change,

159.play: Their needles to lances, and their gentle hearts

161.play: LEWIS: There end thy brave, and turn thy face in peace;

162.play: We grant thou canst outscold us: fare thee well;

163.play: We hold our time too precious to be spent

165.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: Give me leave to speak.

167.play: LEWIS: We will attend to neither.

168.play: Strike up the drums; and let the tongue of war

169.play: Plead for our interest and our being here.

170.play: BASTARD: Indeed your drums, being beaten, will cry out;

171.play: And so shall you, being beaten: do but start

172.play: An echo with the clamour of thy drum,

173.play: And even at hand a drum is ready braced

174.play: That shall reverberate all as loud as thine;

175.play: Sound but another, and another shall

176.play: As loud as thine rattle the welkin's ear

177.play: And mock the deep-mouth'd thunder: for at hand,

178.play: Not trusting to this halting legate here,

179.play: Whom he hath used rather for sport than need

180.play: Is warlike John; and in his forehead sits

181.play: A bare-ribb'd death, whose office is this day

182.play: To feast upon whole thousands of the French.

183.play: LEWIS: Strike up our drums, to find this danger out.

184.play: BASTARD: And thou shalt find it, Dauphin, do not doubt.

78.play: As it on earth hath been thy servant still.

79.play: Now, now, you stars that move in your right spheres,

80.play: Where be your powers? show now your mended faiths,

81.play: And instantly return with me again,

82.play: To push destruction and perpetual shame

83.play: Out of the weak door of our fainting land.

84.play: Straight let us seek, or straight we shall be sought;

85.play: The Dauphin rages at our very heels.

86.play: SALISBURY: It seems you know not, then, so much as we:

87.play: The Cardinal Pandulph is within at rest,

88.play: Who half an hour since came from the Dauphin,

89.play: And brings from him such offers of our peace

90.play: As we with honour and respect may take,

91.play: With purpose presently to leave this war.

92.play: BASTARD: He will the rather do it when he sees

93.play: Ourselves well sinewed to our defence.

94.play: SALISBURY: Nay, it is in a manner done already;

95.play: For many carriages he hath dispatch'd

96.play: To the sea-side, and put his cause and quarrel

97.play: To the disposing of the cardinal:

98.play: With whom yourself, myself and other lords,

99.play: If you think meet, this afternoon will post

101.play: BASTARD: Let it be so: and you, my noble prince,

102.play: With other princes that may best be spared,

103.play: Shall wait upon your father's funeral.

104.play: PRINCE HENRY: At Worcester must his body be interr'd;

107.play: And happily may your sweet self put on

108.play: The lineal state and glory of the land!

109.play: To whom with all submission, on my knee

110.play: I do bequeath my faithful services

112.play: SALISBURY: And the like tender of our love we make,

113.play: To rest without a spot for evermore.

114.play: PRINCE HENRY: I have a kind soul that would give you thanks

115.play: And knows not how to do it but with tears.

116.play: BASTARD: O, let us pay the time but needful woe,

117.play: Since it hath been beforehand with our griefs.

118.play: This England never did, nor never shall,

119.play: Lie at the proud foot of a conqueror,

120.play: But when it first did help to wound itself.

121.play: Now these her princes are come home again,

122.play: Come the three corners of the world in arms,

123.play: And we shall shock them. Nought shall make us rue,

124.play: If England to itself do rest but true.
